Understanding Nerve Agents
Nerve agents are highly toxic substances that affect the nervous system. They work by blocking the action of an enzyme called acetylcholinesterase, which is responsible for breaking down a neurotransmitter called acetylcholine. Without this enzyme, acetylcholine builds up in the body, leading to severe symptoms such as convulsions, paralysis, and death. Nerve agents can be inhaled, ingested, or absorbed through the skin.

There’s an old adage in ethics that says “the ends do not justify the means”. For example, if I need money badly enough to steal from my employer, I might try justifying it by saying that I’m doing it to feed my children. But that doesn’t mean that the act itself is ethical, even if the end goal is. If my children are starving, there are more ethical ways for me to acquire money than stealing from my employer.

However, some ethicists believe that the ends can justify the means. They would say that stealing from my employer was ethical in this instance, because the end result is a family that isn’t starving. As long as the end result is moral and justifiable, then the means don’t matter.

On Aug. 6, 1945, at 8:15 a.m., the United States dropped an atomic bomb on the city of Hiroshima. Three days later, at 11:02 a.m., it dropped a second bomb on Nagasaki. The world was changed forever. Today marks the beginning of that change. On this anniversary, it is appropriate to recall these terrible events and reflect upon their meaning.

For the past 64 years, Americans have been taught to view the atomic attacks through the lens of tragedy. The bombs killed hundreds of thousands of civilians and

it may be time to start thinking differently about them.


On Aug. 6, 1945, a uranium bomb called Little Boy destroyed most of Hiroshima, and a plutonium bomb called Fat Man did the same to Nagasaki three days later. The bombs killed tens of thousands of Japanese instantly. Another 100,000 or more would die in the following weeks and months, mostly civilians. In both cities, the overwhelming majority of those who died were not combatants, nor even supporters of Japan’s brutal imperial war effort. Most were ordinary people who happened to live in the wrong place at the wrong time.

For decades, Americans have treated the atomic bombings as tragic necessities—the price that had to be paid to end the war before an invasion of the Japanese home islands could claim millions more lives, American and Japanese alike. That conventional view has come under assault in recent years from historians who have argued that Japan was on the verge of surrendering, and that the

the victims had no more moral claim on our sympathy than the victims of Pearl Harbor or Dresden.

In a blog post at, I argue that the Japanese victims of Hiroshima and Nagasaki had no more moral claim on our sympathy than did the victims of Pearl Harbor or Dresden. 

This is not an argument in favor of bombing civilians, but rather against the view that the destruction of cities is ipso facto immoral. I note that the atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki were strategically justified, but the same can be said for many other attacks on urban areas during World War II.

Atomic bomb dropped on Hiroshima.

We have a problem with Hiroshima.

For most Americans, the atomic bombing of Hiroshima (and Nagasaki) is a tragedy, one of those necessary evils that occasionally accompanies victory. The bombs were horrible weapons, which killed hundreds of thousands of people, many of them women, children, and other noncombatants. But the alternative would have been an invasion of Japan, which could have cost millions

they were actually justified.

Why did we drop the bomb on Hiroshima and Nagasaki? The standard answer is that it was a necessary evil. The U.S. had to end the war with Japan as soon as possible and an invasion of the Japanese homeland would have cost far more lives than the atomic bombings.

In fact, the bombings were entirely unnecessary and unjustified. Japan was already on the verge of surrender when the bombs were dropped. The only reason to drop them was to demonstrate American power to the Soviets, who had entered the Pacific War just days earlier. As President Truman himself said in his diary on July 25, 1945, “We have discovered the most terrible bomb in the history of the world. It may be the firecracker that will burn the Communists out.”

that’s wrong. The people who died in Hiroshima and Nagasaki deserve their fate.

This year marks 73 years since the atomic bombing of Hiroshima and Nagasaki. This is a good time to reflect on what those bombs meant. They were weapons of mass destruction unleashed on civilians—the ultimate crime against humanity. Those two cities are the only places where nuclear weapons have been used in war, so we are rightly horrified by what happened there. Every August, the media rehashes how tragic it was. But this conventional wisdom, while understandable, is dead wrong.

In fact, the people who died in Hiroshima and Nagasaki deserve their fate. It is not a tragedy that the bombs fell on them. In the context of World War II, there was no better way to deal with them than to atomize them.
